content.boundary: Polish legal-framework and social-capital evidence is moderate-to-strong, but no Hungarian pilot data exist and the Sáta case is a single weak-evidence example, so overall Hungarian transferability remains weak.
content.change_lever: Poland introduced a legal framework (gmina agreements with associations, foundations, or natural persons) allowing small schools (up to 70 students) to remain open under non-governmental management while retaining public funding and curriculum.; Structural social capital decline in villages where primary schools closed (2000-2016) was significantly larger than in villages where schools remained open, as measured by a multiple-case study of four Polish villages.
content.name: Networked retention: clustering, association-run and multi-service hub models
content.system_problem: Retain village primary schools through inter-municipal pedagogical networks (French RPI-style clustering), community/association-run publicly-subsidized schools (Polish model), or multi-service hubs combining school with other civic functions.
